Recovery vaults are concentrated in eastus while VMs are concentrated in westus2, centralindia and southeastasia: because 68.9% of microsoft.recoveryservices/vaults are in eastus but microsoft.compute/virtualmachines are distributed mostly in westus2 (23.9%), centralindia (20.8%) and southeastasia (18%), you should provision Recovery Services vaults (or dataprotection backup vaults) in the same regions as your critical VMs to ensure they can be protected and meet Azure's regional protection requirements.

Vault identity usage is mixed while VMs overwhelmingly have managed identities: microsoft.recoveryservices/vaults show a 50/50 split between UserAssigned and None and microsoft.compute/virtualmachines are 98.8% SystemAssigned+UserAssigned, so align backup vault identities with the tenant's user-assigned managed identity practice and grant least-privilege access to vault MIs when configuring VM protection and restore operations.

Recovery Services vaults have infrastructure encryption enabled: since encryption.infrastructureencryption is Enabled for recovery services vaults, plan long-term retention policies using these encrypted vaults and, where compliance demands customer key control, evaluate adding customer-managed keys to meet audit/key-control requirements.

Storage redundancy is primarily single-region but there is non-trivial geo-redundant usage: with storageaccounts skewing to Standard_LRS (48.5%) but a combined ~40% on Standard_GRS/Standard_RAGRS, choose backup storage/replication settings (or vault storage redundancy options) deliberately—use GRS/RAGRS or vault replication if regulatory long-term retention requires geo-redundancy.

Tenant VMs are overwhelmingly Windows (95.2%): because most protected compute is Windows, design backup jobs and retention around Windows VM characteristics (OS disk sizes, licensing and restore validation) and include quarterly restore tests that validate Windows-specific restore procedures and SLAs.

Disks are mostly premium: microsoft.compute/disks show Premium_LRS (63.2%) and a Premium tier majority, so anticipate higher snapshot/restore cost and performance impact for backups of critical VMs and size retention windows and restore SLAs accordingly to control cost and meet recovery objectives.

Key Vaults enforce strong deletion and purge protections: key vaults have enablesoftdelete True and enablepurgeprotection True with 90-day soft-delete retention common, so integrate those key lifecycle protections into backup encryption key management and ensure retention/purge settings satisfy compliance for long-term backup retention.

Network exposure of backup resources is broad today: recovery services vaults show publicnetworkaccess Enabled for ~98.9% and storage accounts are mixed between Enabled and Disabled, therefore harden backup infrastructure by introducing private endpoints / network restrictions for vaults and validating that backup agents and restore processes have the necessary allowed network paths before enforcing restrictions.
